findings.title: capex-trend.js: Math.abs() on envelope object {value:x} silently yields NaN
findings.description: Lines 21/23 read `const capexT = Math.abs(capexArr[0]);` but `capexArr[0]` is the envelope object `{value: <number>}` (the same shape from which the file extracts `revT = revArr[0] && revArr[0].value` two lines down). Math.abs on an object returns NaN.
findings.mechanism: capexArr[0] is `{value: -200}` (Yahoo capex is reported negative). `Math.abs({value:-200})` -> NaN (object → primitive → NaN). Then `capexT == null` is false (NaN != null), and `revT > 0` checks still pass, so flow proceeds to `const ratioT = capexT / revT` = NaN. Final `value = NaN/NaN = NaN`, and `pass: NaN <= 1.5` = false. Method always returns pass=false with bogus reason string. Because capex-trend is DIAGNOSTIC (not core/dataguard) the corrupt result is hidden, but `_inferConfidence` in _helpers.js and any consumer of `r.value` poisons further math.
findings.reproducer: Pass a stock with `annual.annualCapex = [{value:-200e6},{value:-180e6},{value:-160e6},{value:-150e6}]` and 4 corresponding revs. evaluate() returns `{value: NaN, pass: false, computable: true}`.
findings.impact: capex-trend always fails or reports nonsense; users see false 'Cash-Burn-Frühwarn' fail for every stock. Hidden because DIAGNOSTIC. If ever promoted to CORE or referenced in score-aggregator, it would silently degrade everything.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `Math.abs(capexArr[0])` with `Math.abs(capexArr[0] && capexArr[0].value)`; same for capexT3 on line 23. Or use H.latestAnnual(stock, 'annualCapex') consistently like other methods.

findings.title: sbc-revenue.js: reads envelope object as scalar, NaN division
findings.description: Line 21: `const sbc = sbcArr[0];` — annualSBC entries are the canonical envelope `{value:x}` shape (consistent with annualRev on line 22 which DOES extract `.value`). Then `value = sbc / rev` is `{object} / number` = NaN. Pass check `NaN <= 0.15` is false → silent always-fail.
findings.mechanism: sbcArr[0] = {value: 1.2e9}. `sbc != null` true, `rev = revArr[0].value = number`. Computes `{value:1.2e9} / 4e9` → NaN. `pass: NaN <= THRESHOLD` is false. Result claims computable=true with value=NaN — wrapper buildResult filters value when not finite (sets null), but pass stays false.
findings.reproducer: Snapshot with `annual.annualSBC = [{value: 1e9}, ...]` and `annual.annualRev = [{value: 5e9}, ...]`. evaluate returns `{value: null, pass: false, computable: true}` with reason 'NaN/...%'.
findings.impact: SBC dilution detector is dead code — never fires correctly. False negatives on stocks that genuinely have excessive SBC.
findings.suggested_fix: Change `const sbc = sbcArr[0];` to `const sbc = sbcArr[0] && sbcArr[0].value;` (mirror the rev extraction one line below). Better: use H.latestAnnual(stock,'annualSBC').

findings.title: Systemic array-index misalignment after _arrVals().filter(Number.isFinite)
findings.file: methods/earnings-stability.js, methods/margin-quality.js, methods/premium-compounder-proof.js, methods/reinvestment-rate.js, methods/sloan-ratio.js, methods/hypergrowth-quality-class.js, methods/q-spike-dataguard.js, methods/revenue-shock-guard.js, methods/forecast-contamination-guard.js, methods/quarter-concentration-guard.js, methods/deceleration-guard.js, methods/revenue-volatility-guard.js, methods/estimate-revision-proxy.js
findings.line_range: _arrVals helpers throughout; usage at e.g. margin-quality.js:51-53, premium-compounder-proof.js:67-69/101-103/112-119, reinvestment-rate.js:46/66-72, sloan-ratio.js:49-55, hypergrowth-quality-class.js:67-91, earnings-stability.js:62-64, deceleration-guard.js:27-31, estimate-revision-proxy.js:31-43
findings.description: All methods using the local `_arrVals` pattern `arr.map(v => v == null ? null : (typeof v === 'number' ? v : v.value)).filter(v => Number.isFinite(v))` produce a *compacted* array that no longer aligns with the original fiscal-year index. Code that then iterates two parallel arrays (`revs[i]`, `opIncs[i]`) or pairs by index assumes the i-th entries are the same year — but if any year has a missing/null value in either source, indices shift and you silently compare year-2024 OpInc with year-2023 Revenue.
findings.mechanism: Example: stock has annualRev=[2024:5B, 2023:4B, 2022:null, 2021:3B] and annualOpInc=[2024:1B, 2023:0.8B, 2022:0.6B, 2021:0.5B]. After _arrVals: revs=[5B,4B,3B] (len 3, 2022 dropped), opIncs=[1B,0.8B,0.6B,0.5B] (len 4). Loop `for i=0..min(5,3,3)=3`: pairs (5B,1B), (4B,0.8B), (3B,0.6B) — last pair is 2021-rev vs 2022-opInc. Margin computed is wrong. Same root cause hits sloan-ratio (nis vs fcfs vs assetsArr — assetsArr is NOT filtered, others are, so years drift differently), hypergrowth-quality-class (revQ[i] vs revQ[i+4] assumes 8 contiguous quarters), revenue-shock-guard (median of 'prior' quarters skewed).
findings.reproducer: Construct a stock where annualOpInc has a null/NaN in year 2 but annualRev has a real value; or quarterly revenueQ with a missing Q. Run margin-quality. The reported gmMedian and opMargMedian will use cross-year pairs.
findings.impact: Silent margin-quality false-passes/false-fails. Sloan-Ratio can be computed against wrong totalAssets year (since assetsArr is taken raw via H.val, not filtered). Reinvestment-rate divides capex of one year by ocf of another. quality-compounder-roic relies on H.latestAnnual which is safe, but premium-compounder-proof rebuilds via _arrVals and IS affected (lines 67-69, 101-103, 112-119). Affects core QC scoring → wrong picks.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) keep parallel arrays in original ordering using `arr.map(v => v == null ? null : (typeof v === 'number' ? v : v.value))` and check nulls per-index without filtering, OR (b) build a single zipped array of records keyed by fiscal year before any per-index pairing. Replace _arrVals usage when index-alignment matters.
findings.compound_hint: Pairs with F-ME-013 (Sloan denominator uses raw assetsArr while numerator uses filtered nis/fcfs).

findings.title: altman-z-score.js: missing-NI silently treated as 0, inflating Z″
findings.description: Lines 60-62: `const ni0 = _annualVal(niArr, 0) || 0;` and same for ni1, ni2. The `|| 0` swallows nulls (no NI data) AND legitimate net-income-of-zero. For a 'retained-earnings proxy' (X2), a missing year is silently treated as 'made zero profit', not as 'data unavailable'.
findings.mechanism: Stock with only 1 year of NI data: ni0=actual, ni1=null→0, ni2=null→0. retainedEarningsProxy = ni0 + 0 + 0 = ni0. X2 calculation pretends the company had 0 retained earnings in years it actually had losses. For distressed companies (turnaround candidates which is precisely the use case), missing historical NI biases X2 UP, inflating Altman Z″ above 1.1 → falsely passes the 'not in distress' threshold. Hard MUST in TURNAROUND mode.
findings.reproducer: Pass a stock with annualNetIncome=[{value:50e6}] only and rest of inputs valid. retainedEarningsProxy=50e6 (treats 2 missing years as 0). With same totalAssets a distressed company gets Z″ pushed safely above 1.1.
findings.impact: TURNAROUND mode passes distressed companies that should fail Altman. Since altman is required:true must in TURNAROUND, this is the primary solvency gate.
findings.suggested_fix: Distinguish missing from zero: only sum ni values that are non-null and Number.isFinite. If <2 years available, return computable:false with insufficient-history reason. At minimum require ni0 to be present.

findings.title: quarterly-earnings-stability: fixed THRESHOLD=6 not scaled when <8 quarters available
findings.description: THRESHOLD=6 is compared to `positive` count even when only 4–7 quarters of NI data exist. A stock with 5 profitable quarters out of 5 (100% profitability record) fails `5 >= 6`. Method allows `ts.length < 4` only as the incomputable gate; in [4,8) the count cap is wrong.
findings.mechanism: If timeseries.netIncomeQ has 5 quarters all positive: window=5, positive=5, pass=5>=6=false. Reports '5 / 5 Quartale NI > 0' — visibly absurd. Compare to piotroski-f-score.js which DOES scale (line 122: `Math.ceil(THRESHOLD * signals.length / 8)`).
findings.reproducer: Snapshot with `timeseries.netIncomeQ = [{value:1},{value:1},{value:1},{value:1},{value:1}]` returns pass=false.
findings.impact: False negatives on stocks with 4-7 quarters of consistently positive earnings — likely many recently-IPO'd or small-cap stocks. Method is DIAGNOSTIC so impact is bounded, but it taints any UI that displays per-method status.
findings.suggested_fix: Scale threshold proportionally: `const scaled = Math.ceil(THRESHOLD * window.length / 8); pass = positive >= scaled;` and return scaled as `threshold` in buildResult.

findings.title: peg.js: short-circuit `H.metricValue(stock,'pe') || H.metricValue(stock,'forwardPE')` falls back when pe=0
findings.description: Operator-precedence/falsy bug: `pe || forwardPE` returns forwardPE whenever pe is 0 (typically means earnings/share=0, technically infinite PE not zero, but Yahoo sometimes reports 0). The fallback masks data quality issues.
findings.mechanism: If Yahoo returns pe={value:0}, metricValue returns 0, falsy short-circuit picks up forwardPE. The two represent different earnings basis (trailing vs forward) — mixing them silently is a category error. Subsequent `pe <= 0` filter catches the case if BOTH are 0 or negative, so the runtime symptom is limited but the value used could be from a different earnings horizon than the user expects.
findings.reproducer: Snapshot with metrics.pe={value:0}, metrics.forwardPE={value:25}, revenueGrowthYoY={value:30}. peg returns 25/30=0.83 pass=true, but ostensibly via trailing pe — user thinks the actual trailing PE is 25.
findings.impact: Misleading components — `components.pe` shows the forwardPE value but is labeled as `pe`. Low signal mixing in PEG calculation if PE=0 happens.
findings.suggested_fix: Use explicit null check: `const pe = H.metricValue(stock,'pe'); const fpe = H.metricValue(stock,'forwardPE'); const usedPe = (pe != null && pe > 0) ? pe : fpe;` and surface which was used in components.

findings.title: net-debt-ebitda threshold (3) inconsistent with QC mode comment (<=2.5) and red-flag rule (>4)
findings.description: Three different thresholds for the same metric coexist: method file has THRESHOLD=3 (pass at <=3), strategy-modes.js QUALITY_COMPOUNDER mustResult declares storyHint 'Net-Debt/EBITDA <= 2.5', and score-aggregator RED_FLAG_RULES says >4 is HIGH_DEBT.
findings.mechanism: When QC evaluates `net-debt-ebitda` it relies on the method's `pass` field, which uses 3. The mode story says 2.5. So a stock with ND/EBITDA=2.8 passes the must-filter but the story is wrong. Red-Flag triggers only above 4 — meaning the [3,4] zone fails the must but is NOT red-flagged.
findings.reproducer: Stock with ND/EBITDA=2.8 passes QC must, story claims 2.5; stock with 3.5 fails must but no red flag is added.
findings.impact: User-visible label discrepancy + silent score gap in 3-4 range. Tier classification inconsistent across mode boundaries.
findings.suggested_fix: Pick one threshold and propagate: either set method THRESHOLD=2.5 to match QC's intent (or override per-mode), and adjust red-flag to >3.5 or align with method threshold.

findings.title: sector-medians-compute.js: median includes negative ROIC/ROCE without sign filter
findings.description: Bucket-push logic only checks `if (ni != null && ta != null)` and `ic > 0` (invested capital positive). If netIncome is negative (loss), the ROIC value pushed is negative. Median is taken over the resulting array, so a sector with 50% loss-makers gets a negative median threshold pushed into sector-medians-auto.json. Downstream effectiveThreshold() then uses this negative number as the pass bar, making roic >= median trivially true for any profitable company.
findings.mechanism: Negative-NI companies push e.g. -0.20 into roic array. Median of [0.05, -0.02, -0.15, 0.10, -0.20] = -0.02. fcf-yield method uses effectiveThreshold(); pass = value >= -0.02 — basically any non-deeply-negative FCF passes.
findings.reproducer: Compute medians on a snapshot set with predominantly loss-making sub-profile (e.g. clinical-stage biotech bucket). Result: medians map contains negative ROIC/ROCE/fcf-yield entries. Then any biotech with FCF/MCAP=-0.01 passes.
findings.impact: Sector-relative scoring becomes inverted in loss-heavy sectors. Lookup uses regional/global buckets so this propagates region-specific corruption.
findings.suggested_fix: Filter to positive contributors before median, or compute median over the *log-distance from 0* using only winsorized data; alternatively require `ni > 0` before pushing into roic bucket and exclude negative ratios for the relative threshold use case.

findings.title: sector-medians-compute: 0 or 1-stock sub-profiles skipped only by MIN check, but rolling12m caches them
findings.description: _computeFromBuckets correctly drops sub-profiles with <minN samples (so 0/1-stock buckets are not written to auto-medians). HOWEVER, writeRollingMedians (line 188) operates on the legacy slice and iterates only over what was written — fine — but if auto-medians is rerun on a different snapshot where a sub-profile dropped below minN, the prior rolling entry remains: `entry.values.filter(v => v.asOf !== today && v.asOf >= cutoff)` keeps old datapoints. The rolling12mMedian is then computed over stale values without any fresh datapoint anchoring. If a bucket evaporates completely from auto-medians on a given run, the rolling history is NOT updated for that bucket at all → stale rolling median that effectiveThreshold() will still prefer (Priority 1 in _helpers.js line 142).
findings.mechanism: Day N: SAAS bucket has 30 stocks → writes median 0.15 to rolling. Day N+1: SAAS classification edge-case drops to 4 stocks (below MIN_STOCKS_PER_SECTOR=5) → autoMedians has no SAAS entry → writeRollingMedians never enters the SAAS loop → SAAS rolling entry untouched, asOf still N-1, but values array still has the last 12 weeks. _helpers.effectiveThreshold checks `rEntry.values.length >= 12` and uses it.
findings.reproducer: Drop one snapshot's classification such that SAAS bucket has 4 stocks. Rerun: sector-medians-rolling.json still contains SAAS.roic from the previous run. effectiveThreshold returns rolling12m source even though current data was insufficient.
findings.impact: Silent reliance on stale medians. Methods report `source: 'rolling12m:SAAS'` confidence-implying-currency even when current run skipped that bucket.
findings.suggested_fix: writeRollingMedians should also EXPIRE entries when their last-update is older than e.g. 14 days, OR explicitly enumerate prior buckets and skip-with-null any that aren't fresh today.

findings.title: score-aggregator.normalizeMethodScore: division by zero threshold returns 0.3 only if threshold IS the number 0
findings.description: Line 94 only escapes if threshold is non-numeric. Numeric threshold of 0 (e.g. `threshold: 0`) leads to division by zero: `op==='gte': ratio = val / 0 = ±Infinity`. The ratio chain checks `ratio >= 0.9` etc. — Infinity passes branch 1, returns `Math.min(1.0, 0.7 + (Infinity - 0.9) * 3) = 1.0`. So any failing method with threshold=0 (e.g. profitability-trend rank=0 case, or a custom 0-threshold) is silently scored 1.0.
findings.mechanism: Method with threshold=0, op='gte', value=10, pass=false (somehow). normalizeMethodScore: val=10, threshold=0, numeric? yes. ratio=10/0=Infinity. ratio>=0.9 branch matches → returns 1.0. Method gets full credit despite pass=false.
findings.reproducer: Mock methodResult={computable:true, pass:false, value:10, threshold:0}; methodMeta={threshold:0, thresholdOp:'gte'}. normalizeMethodScore returns 1.0.
findings.impact: If any method ever exports threshold:0 (which is plausible — e.g. a 'X >= 0' check), score gets max credit on FAIL. Currently no method does this, but the precondition is fragile.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `if (threshold === 0) return val > 0 ? 1.0 : 0;` or guard explicit division-by-zero before computing ratio.

findings.title: score-aggregator: lte ratio inverts incorrectly when val is negative
findings.description: Lines 100-103: for `op === 'lte'`: `if (val <= 0) return 0.3; ratio = threshold / val;`. This wrongly penalizes legitimately *better-than-zero* values. For metrics where lower is better with a positive threshold (e.g. peg threshold 1.5, value=0.0 means perfect or undefined), the code returns 0.3 instead of 1.0. Also: for metrics where negative is legitimate (net-debt-ebitda value=-2 means net cash — excellent!), the function returns 0.3 (mediocre) rather than 1.0.
findings.mechanism: net-debt-ebitda for net-cash company: value=-1.0 (debt < cash), pass=true. normalizeMethodScore early-returns 1.0 because pass=true. OK that path is safe. But Sloan-Ratio (op='lte_abs') uses op fallback `else return 0.3;` (line 104) — so any failing Sloan-Ratio entry maps to 0.3 regardless of how close to threshold. This blunts the graduated scoring.
findings.reproducer: Sloan-Ratio failing at value=0.21 (just over 0.20 threshold) returns score 0.3. Same as value=0.95. No gradation for lte_abs op.
findings.impact: Sloan-Ratio and any other `lte_abs` method skips graduated scoring entirely. Score-Aggregator's whole 'near threshold' design is bypassed for those methods.
findings.suggested_fix: Add explicit handler for `lte_abs`: `ratio = threshold / Math.abs(val)`. Also handle val<=0 in lte case more carefully (val<0 might be excellent).

findings.title: score-aggregator graduated mapping discontinuity at boundaries
findings.description: Mapping piecewise-linear functions: `ratio>=0.9 → 0.7 + (ratio-0.9)*3`, `ratio>=0.7 → 0.3 + (ratio-0.7)*2`. At ratio=0.9 exactly: branch 1 returns 0.7 + 0 = 0.7; if ratio just below 0.9 (0.8999): branch 2 returns 0.3 + (0.8999-0.7)*2 = 0.6998. Consistent. But branch 1 at ratio=1.0 returns 0.7 + 0.3 = 1.0 — also consistent. Wait: at ratio=0.9 actual: Math.min(1.0, 0.7+0*3)=0.7. So no discontinuity. However at boundary ratio=1.0 the branch jumps directly to pass=true at the call site, so this branch is only entered for ratio in [0.9, ~] when pass=false (i.e. the threshold direction means ratio<1 but val crossed via op). The pass=true short-circuit at line 84 covers most. Real risk: ratio could be > 1 if value just barely fails (e.g. val=99 vs threshold=100, op=gte → ratio=0.99, branch 1, ~0.97). OK. Discontinuity is bounded.
findings.mechanism: More subtle: ratio=0.7 exactly: branch 2 returns 0.3+0=0.3; just below: branch 3 returns 0.1+0*1=0.1 — discontinuous jump of 0.2 at ratio=0.7. Same at ratio=0.5: branch 3 returns 0.1; just below: branch 4 returns max(0, 0.499*0.2)=0.0998 — discontinuous jump of ~0.0002 (negligible). Main 0.7 boundary is real (0.2 jump).
findings.reproducer: Two stocks with ratio=0.7 and 0.699 receive scores 0.30 and 0.10 respectively — 3x difference for 0.1% data delta.
findings.impact: Tier-boundary stocks can flip A↔B based on noise.
findings.suggested_fix: Use continuous piecewise function or smooth via sigmoid.

findings.title: sloan-ratio: nis/fcfs filtered by Number.isFinite but assetsArr is raw; year-misalignment
findings.description: Line 34: `nis = _arrVals(...)` filters nulls. Line 35: `fcfs = _arrVals(...)` filters nulls. Line 36: `assetsArr = H.val(stock,'annual.annualBalance')` is the RAW array (no filtering). The loop then uses `i` as a shared index across all three.
findings.mechanism: If annualNetIncome=[{value:1B},null,{value:0.8B},{value:0.6B}] and annualBalance=[{tA:10B},{tA:9B},{tA:8B},{tA:7B}] and annualFCF=[{value:0.9B},{value:0.7B},{value:0.5B},{value:0.4B}]: nis=[1B,0.8B,0.6B] (year-2 dropped), fcfs=[0.9B,0.7B,0.5B,0.4B], assetsArr length=4. yearsAvailable=min(3,4,4)=3. Loop i=0..2 pairs (nis[0]=1B, fcfs[0]=0.9B, assetsArr[0].totalAssets=10B) — OK; (nis[1]=0.8B which is YEAR 2 actually, fcfs[1]=year 1, assetsArr[1].totalAssets=year 1 → mixed-year sloan). Year-2 data quietly drops from nis but the index still maps to year-1's fcf and balance.
findings.reproducer: Snapshot with one null in annualNetIncome year-2 (common when Yahoo missed a filing). Sloan computed mixes years.
findings.impact: DataGuard 'sloan-ratio' can wrongly fire OR pass. Used as MUST in QC and other modes. Quality of the entire Sloan-based hygiene layer is suspect on stocks with missing annual data.
findings.suggested_fix: Build a single per-year-record list before computing: zip indices first, drop the whole record if any of (ni, fcf, totalAssets) is missing.
findings.compound_hint: Same root cause as F-ME-003 but isolated here because of mixed filtering policies.

findings.title: hypergrowth-quality-class: revQ[i+4] YoY pairing assumes 8 contiguous quarters; _arr filter breaks this
findings.description: Loop at line 69: `const cur = revQ[i], prev = revQ[i + 4];` assumes that the 8-element revQ is 8 contiguous quarters in chronological order. But revQ comes from `_arr(stock,'timeseries.revenueQ')` which filters non-finite — so if Q-4 is missing, all subsequent index positions are shifted. Length check `>=8` does not detect interior gaps.
findings.mechanism: Stock with revenueQ=[Q1=1,Q0=0.95,null,Q-2=0.9,Q-3=0.85,Q-4=0.8,Q-5=0.75,Q-6=0.7,Q-7=0.65,Q-8=0.6]. After _arr: [1, 0.95, 0.9, 0.85, 0.8, 0.75, 0.7, 0.65, 0.6] (9 elements). i=0 pair (1, 0.8) — was supposed to be (Q0, Q-4) = (1, 0.85). Off by one. YoY rate wrong → wrong strongQ/solidQ count → wrong classification.
findings.reproducer: Inject a null into timeseries.revenueQ[2]. evaluate() returns different class than with full data.
findings.impact: Hypergrowth-quality-class is CORE and used in HYPERGROWTH mode score weight 0.15. Misclassification (REAL vs Q_SPIKE_FAKE) on stocks with any Q-revenue gap.
findings.suggested_fix: Use raw revenueQ array preserving nulls, only do YoY math when both cur and prev are non-null and prev>0.

findings.title: _helpers.metricValue silently returns null for {value:null}; downstream treats as 'missing'
findings.description: metricValue: `if (typeof m === 'object' && 'value' in m) return m.value;` — returns m.value even when m.value is null/undefined/NaN. Callers then do `if (val == null) return notComputable` which catches null but NOT NaN. Some metrics later get arithmetic on NaN (rule-of-40: `growth + fcfMargin` where one might be NaN if snapshot wrote {value:NaN}).
findings.mechanism: If a metric envelope has {value:NaN}, metricValue returns NaN. `growth == null` is false. `value = NaN + fcfMargin` = NaN. pass=NaN>=40=false. computable=true silently. Reason string shows 'NaN'.
findings.reproducer: Snapshot with metrics.revenueGrowthYoY={value:NaN}. rule-of-40 returns computable=true, value=NaN, pass=false.
findings.impact: False fails marked as computable. wrapEvaluate's buildResult does `value: value != null && Number.isFinite(value) ? value : null` so the value field is sanitized to null but pass and reason are not.
findings.suggested_fix: metricValue should return null on non-finite values; or treat NaN as missing in callers.

findings.title: drawdown-52w/high-proximity-52w: 'last 252 days' may be calendar gap, not trading days
findings.description: Methods assume series is daily trading-day-spaced. If history.json contains weekly or irregular cadence (or if a stock has trading halts), slice(-252) is not the last 52 weeks.
findings.mechanism: Stock with weekly history: slice(-252) is 252 weeks ≈ 5 years, not 52 weeks. High52w then reflects 5-year high, drawdown looks artificially worse.
findings.reproducer: Stock whose prices/history.json entry is weekly-sampled. Drawdown values much larger than expected.
findings.impact: Cross-asset comparability broken; drawdown thresholds intended for 1y span apply to multi-year span.
findings.suggested_fix: Filter by date range explicitly (last 365 calendar days) instead of count.

findings.title: wrapEvaluate confidence/flags reference `result.threshold` but threshold may be a string
findings.description: _inferConfidence and _autoFlags both check `result.threshold != null && result.threshold !== 0` then compute `(result.value - result.threshold) / result.threshold`. For profitability-state (threshold='TURNAROUND'), profitability-trend ('FLAT'), the threshold is a string. `(num - 'TURNAROUND') / 'TURNAROUND'` = NaN. Conditional check `dist < 0.05` is false so NEAR_THRESHOLD flag not added — silent skip OK. But score-aggregator (F-ME-003 in part) at line 94 DOES guard non-numeric threshold; this guard is only in score-aggregator, not in _helpers. Inconsistent.
findings.mechanism: Confidence subtraction `c -= 0.1` skipped when dist is NaN (false branch). Acceptable but unintentional.
findings.reproducer: Run wrapEvaluate on profitability-state result; observe `dist` evaluated as NaN, no NEAR_THRESHOLD flag.
findings.impact: Loss of NEAR_THRESHOLD flag for state-based methods. Minor UX impact.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `typeof result.threshold === 'number'` guard before the dist calculation.

findings.title: stock.meta.region: schema mismatch between modes-report (exchange-like) and region-mapping (region-bucket)
findings.description: generate-modes-report.js reads `stock.meta.region` as one of 'Nasdaq','NYSE','XETRA','LSE',... (exchange names mapped via REGION_TO_COUNTRY). region-mapping.js treats `stock.meta.region` as priority-1 explicit override expecting 'US'/'EU'/'APAC'/'EM'. Both can't be right.
findings.mechanism: If a pull writes meta.region='Nasdaq', region-mapping returns 'Nasdaq' (because explicitRegion is truthy and returned as-is), then sector-median-lookup looks up `medians.byRegion['Nasdaq']` which never exists → falls back to _GLOBAL. Regional calibration is silently disabled for every US stock.
findings.reproducer: Snapshot with meta.region='NYSE'. getRegion returns 'NYSE'. lookupMedian byRegion['NYSE'] undefined → only _GLOBAL used.
findings.impact: Tag 167 regional calibration mostly never activates. Methods that depend on regional medians (roic, fcf-yield) use global thresholds regardless of region.
findings.suggested_fix: Either: (a) generate-modes-report should use stock.meta.exchange to derive country and ignore region as bucket; (b) region-mapping should validate that explicit region is one of ['US','EU','APAC','EM','OTHER'] before accepting it.

findings.title: generate-modes-report renderCard uses `revGrowth > -100` sentinel test that fails when revGrowth=-100
findings.description: Lines 295-296 default fcfMargin/revGrowth to -999 sentinel. Line 330: `revGrowth > -100 ? formatted : '—'`. If a real revGrowth value is exactly -100 (-100% YoY decline), it's rendered as '—' (missing). Same for fcfMargin at -100.
findings.mechanism: Sentinel -999 distinguishes 'missing' from 'real'. Threshold -100 used to filter shows '—' for both missing AND value of -100. Edge case but conceivable for a company going to ~0 revenue.
findings.reproducer: Stock with rule-of-40.components.growth=-100. UI shows '—' instead of '-100%'.
findings.impact: Edge-case data lost in card rendering.
findings.suggested_fix: Test against the sentinel directly: `revGrowth !== -999 ? ... : '—'`.

findings.title: premium-compounder-proof: computable flag logic uses `c.val != null` after `c1..c6` already converted to booleans
findings.description: Line 127: `computable: checks.every(c => c.val != null) || passing > 0`. The .val fields ARE the raw numeric (rev5yCagr, opMed, etc.) — many of them WILL be null when corresponding annual rows are missing. So if even ONE check has val=null AND no other check passes, the method is incomputable. But the second clause `passing > 0` flips to computable any time at least one check passes — meaning a stock where 1 of 6 succeed is reported as computable=true even when most data is missing.
findings.mechanism: Stock with annualRev only (no opIncs, no fcfs, etc.): c1=true val=0.25; c2..c6 val=null pass=false. checks.every(c.val!=null)=false. passing=1>0 → computable=true. The output's pass=false (allPass=false) with reason 'Premium-Proof FAIL: 1/6'. Misleading: missing data conflated with negative finding.
findings.reproducer: Stock missing annualOpInc, annualFCF, annualBalance entirely but with 5y annualRev: returns computable=true, pass=false, value=1.
findings.impact: False signal that the proof was applied and failed — instead of correctly reporting incomputable.
findings.suggested_fix: `computable: checks.every(c => c.val != null)` only; or report number of computable checks and require >= 4 for the verdict.

findings.title: earnings-stability: recovery test uses unaligned indices after filter
findings.description: _arrVals filters non-finite from opIncWindow (line 40 slice from opInc which was already filtered). The 'older' (i+1) entry is intended to be the prior fiscal year, but after filtering, [i+1] could be 2 calendar years back if year-between was null. Recovery test 'next > declined * 1.2' may compare unrelated years.
findings.mechanism: Stock with opInc=[5B, null (year-1 missing), 3B, 2.5B]. After filter: [5B, 3B, 2.5B]. Loop i=0..1. (newer=5B, older=3B) → decline = -67%. maxDecline=0.667 fails 50% gate immediately. Recovery test not even reached. But for less-extreme case with all 5 present except year-2: filtered [5B,4.5B,3B,2.5B], loop pairs (5B,4.5B)(4.5B,3B)(3B,2.5B). Year-1 vs year-3 comparison labeled as year-over-year decline.
findings.reproducer: Snapshot with annualOpInc missing year-2. Earnings-stability computes 'recovery' across the gap.
findings.impact: Edge case under typical Yahoo data. Affects QC MUST 1.
findings.suggested_fix: Preserve nulls in the windowed array; skip pair when newer or older is null.

findings.title: evaluateMode acceptValues path returns even when r.pass is true, possibly conflicting state
findings.description: When `check.acceptValues` is set and `r.components.state||trend` exists, evaluateMode skips the standard r.pass check entirely. But a method may return r.pass=true with a state value NOT in acceptValues (e.g., profitability-state returns state='STABLE' which is rank>=1 so pass=true, but TURNAROUND mode only accepts ['TURNAROUND','RECENT']). The acceptValues filter dominates, so STABLE stocks are kept OUT of TURNAROUND mode despite passing the method's own threshold. Probably intended, but it means the visibility 'pass' flag on the method conflicts with the mode's classification.
findings.mechanism: STABLE company: profitability-state.pass=true. In TURNAROUND mode evaluateMode loop: r.components.state='STABLE', acceptValues=['TURNAROUND','RECENT'] → status='fail'. So the mustResults[].status says 'fail' even though method itself passed.
findings.reproducer: Eval TURNAROUND mode on AAPL (STABLE). mustResults for profitability-state: status='fail'. Method-level pass=true.
findings.impact: User-visible inconsistency between method's pass flag and mode's must status. Likely intended but underdocumented.
findings.suggested_fix: Document the precedence; or rename mustResult.status to reflect mode-filter outcome distinctly from method pass.

findings.title: score-aggregator total weight normalization: missing methods omitted from totalWeight, inflating remaining weights
findings.description: totalWeight accumulates across all weighted methods including those with incomputable results (s=0). Then normScore = (weightedSum/totalWeight)*100. An incomputable method contributes weight to denominator but 0 to numerator. Result: if half of QC methods are incomputable (small-cap with no balance sheet), the score is artificially halved rather than computed over only computable signals.
findings.mechanism: QC: 8 methods, weights sum to 1.0. Suppose 4 of them are incomputable (snapshot missing); each contributes 0 score with combined weight 0.5. weightedSum = (sum of 4 computable) which max would be 0.5. normScore = 0.5/1.0*100=50. A stock that passes all 4 computable methods is capped at score 50 (NEAR_MISS), not A-tier.
findings.reproducer: Stock missing annualOpInc → quality-compounder-roic, margin-quality, earnings-stability, reinvestment-rate all incomputable. Remaining 4 methods all pass. Score = (1.0+0+0.05+0.05+0.05+0.05+0+0)/1.0 * 100 ≈ 25 = REJECT, despite no failures.
findings.impact: Small / international stocks with partial financial data are pushed into NEAR_MISS or REJECT regardless of quality — invisible cohort bias.
findings.suggested_fix: Normalize against only computable weight: `(weightedSum / sumOfComputableWeights) * 100`. Or apply a minimum-coverage threshold before scoring.
findings.compound_hint: Compounds with F-ME-003 since misaligned data → more methods incomputable → more cohort bias.

findings.title: modes-report main writes to ./pipeline-health relative to CWD, not script location
findings.description: `const healthDir = './pipeline-health';` is relative to process.cwd(), but most other paths in this script (loadStocks) use --snapshots arg which is also relative. If the script is invoked from a different cwd than repo root, pipeline-health is written to wrong location.
findings.mechanism: Run from a parent dir: pipeline-health/ created under parent. CI workflow consumer expects fixed path under repo root.
findings.reproducer: cd /tmp && node /path/to/screener-data/generate-modes-report.js — pipeline-health/ created in /tmp.
findings.impact: CI workflow may not find pipeline-health.json.
findings.suggested_fix: Use `path.join(__dirname, 'pipeline-health')` or anchor to repo root.
